era_context: The mid-12th century continues with the consolidation of major powers: the Angevin Empire in Western Europe, the Zengid dynasty in the Islamic Near East, the Byzantine Empire under Manuel I, the Jin Dynasty in northern China, and the Song Dynasty in the south. This is a period of relative stability in many regions, though underlying tensions are building that will lead to major conflicts in the coming decades.

events.title: Hogen Rebellion in Japan; Taira Clan Victory
events.description: The Hogen Rebellion (Hogen no Ran) erupted in 1156 as a conflict between rival branches of the imperial family and their samurai supporters. The Taira clan, led by Taira no Kiyomori, defeated the Minamoto clan and their allies, establishing Taira dominance at the imperial court. This rebellion marked a crucial step in the rise of the samurai warrior class and the decline of the Heian aristocracy. The victory gave the Taira clan unprecedented power and set the stage for their dominance of Japanese politics until the Gempei War (1180–1185).

sources.name: Hogen Monogatari (Tale of the Hogen Rebellion)
Historiographic consensus on Japanese medieval period

events.certainty_note: The Hogen Rebellion of 1156 is well-documented in Japanese sources. The date and significance are not disputed.
cross_references: Precedes the Heiji Rebellion (1159) and the Gempei War (1180–1185)

events.title: Henry II of England Campaigns in Normandy and Anjou
events.description: Henry II continued to consolidate his control over his vast continental holdings in Normandy and Anjou. By 1156, he had secured his position in England and was actively managing his French territories, which were threatened by the growing power of the French king Louis VII. Henry's campaigns and diplomatic efforts in Normandy and Anjou were part of his broader strategy to maintain the Angevin Empire against French royal pressure.
key_figures: Henry II (King of England, Duke of Normandy)
events.certainty_note: Henry II's campaigns in Normandy and Anjou are attested in chronicles, though specific details of individual campaigns in 1156 are less detailed than major conflicts.
cross_references: Part of Henry II's broader consolidation of the Angevin Empire

events.title: Nur ad-Din's Consolidation of Zengid Power; Expansion of Islamic Education
events.description: Nur ad-Din ibn Zengi continued to consolidate his power in Syria and Mesopotamia, and by 1156, he was actively promoting Islamic education and culture. He founded madrasas (Islamic schools) and supported scholars, contributing to a broader Islamic cultural revival. This period saw the development of a more organized Islamic military and administrative structure that would eventually enable Saladin to unify the Muslim Near East.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)

events.certainty_note: Nur ad-Din's cultural and educational initiatives are well-attested in Islamic sources, though specific events of 1156 are less detailed than major military campaigns.
cross_references: Part of Nur ad-Din's broader consolidation of power (1146–1174)

events.title: Song Dynasty Maritime Trade and Economic Development
events.region: Southern Song China, South China Sea
events.description: The Southern Song Dynasty continued to develop its maritime trade networks and economic infrastructure. By 1156, Song merchants were actively engaged in trade with Southeast Asia, India, and the Middle East. The Song Dynasty's focus on maritime commerce and naval power was a response to the loss of northern China to the Jin Dynasty and represented a shift in economic strategy toward sea-based trade rather than overland routes.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Song Dynasty's maritime expansion is well-documented in Chinese sources, though specific events of 1156 are less detailed than broader trends.
cross_references: Part of the Song Dynasty's economic and technological development (960–1279)

disconfirming_evidence: The Hogen Rebellion is sometimes presented as a decisive turning point in Japanese history, but modern scholarship suggests it was one of several conflicts that gradually shifted power from the aristocracy to the warrior class. The significance of the rebellion is sometimes overstated; the Taira clan's dominance was not inevitable and was challenged by the Minamoto clan in subsequent conflicts. Henry II's campaigns in Normandy and Anjou are sometimes presented as uniformly successful, but he faced significant challenges from French royal pressure and internal conflicts.
historiographic_note: The year 1156 is well-documented in Japanese sources (the Heike Monogatari and related chronicles) and moderately well-documented in Western European sources (English and Norman chronicles). Chinese sources provide information on Song Dynasty economic development. The surviving record emphasizes political and military events; economic, cultural, and technological developments are underrepresented except in Japanese and Chinese sources.

graph_edges.note: The Hogen Rebellion's outcome set the stage for the Heiji Rebellion
The Hogen Rebellion was part of the broader conflict between Taira and Minamoto clans
Henry II's consolidation in 1156 was part of the broader Angevin period
Nur ad-Din's consolidation in 1156 was part of his broader reign
